does: This paper conducts a systematic red-teaming evaluation of Anthropic's Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 language models using the HackAgent framework to test adversarial robustness against jailbreak attacks. The researchers generate hundreds of thousands of automated adversarial attempts across four attack families targeting 7,826 harmful intents mapped to a ten-category harm taxonomy. Every apparent success is independently re-adjudicated by a panel of three judge models using majority voting. The study reveals that while both models resist most attacks, adaptive iterative attacks—particularly tree-of-attacks search—remain effective, breaking Opus 4.8 on 11.5% of intents and Fable 5 on 6.1%. The red-teaming framework successfully locates over 2,300 panel-confirmed harmful completions spanning all harm categories, demonstrating that aggregate success rates mask important vulnerabilities in the residual attack surface.
connects: This work builds on growing literature in adversarial robustness evaluation for large language models, extending previous research on jailbreak attacks and automated red-teaming methodologies. It responds to limitations in aggregate safety metrics by providing fine-grained analysis of which attack families succeed and fail, connecting to work on adaptive optimization for finding model failures and research distinguishing between static obfuscation and iterative search strategies. The study leverages procedurally generated red-teaming frameworks similar to prior work on systematic vulnerability discovery, while introducing multi-judge adjudication to address reliability concerns in harm classification. This research builds on the growing recognition that frontier models require continuous adversarial evaluation and that simple attack success rates can obscure meaningful gaps in safety training.
enables: This comprehensive red-teaming study provides a template for systematic vulnerability assessment of frontier LLMs and identifies tree-of-attacks search as a particularly effective adversarial method warranting further mitigation research. The findings enable developers to prioritize defenses against adaptive attacks over static obfuscation, informing where safety training efforts should concentrate. The panel-adjudicated dataset of confirmed failures across all harm categories provides empirical grounding for future work on understanding failure modes and developing targeted hardening strategies. The results suggest promising directions for stronger adversarial training, better detection of adaptive attack patterns, and development of robust defense mechanisms against iterative optimization. The study also enables more honest communication about safety limitations of frontier models and supports research into whether certain types of harm are fundamentally harder to defend against.
